topic: Describing Emotions and Figurative Language Review
title: Describing Emotions and Figurative Language Review
explanation_chunks: Words can show exactly how someone is feeling! We've got lots of adjectives, like 'blithe' (which means super happy!) or 'grouchy' (which means grumpy), to describe different moods. Using these specific words makes your writing much more colourful.

Remember those cool poetic tricks? 'Alliteration' is when words start with the same sound, making a fun rhythm. 'Rhyme' is when words at the end of lines sound similar, and 'personification' is when you give human actions to things that aren't human.

We also have 'idioms'. These are special phrases where the words don't mean exactly what they say. For example, 'every cloud has a silver lining' doesn't mean actual clouds, but that there's always something good to find even in a tough situation.
Understanding these language tools helps you write more expressively and also appreciate the clever ways poets like Grace Nichols (who wrote our 'Sun is laughing' poem!) choose their words.
examples: Idiom: If you're having a bad day, a friend might say, 'Don't worry, *every cloud has a silver lining*,' meaning things will get better.
Personification: 'The *storm seized the sky* and *tore it to shreds*.'
key_points: Use varied adjectives to describe feelings accurately.
Recall and practice alliteration, rhyme, and personification.
Understand idioms as phrases with non-literal meanings.
Recognise the impact of figurative language in writing.

vocabulary.definition: full of energy, excitement, and cheerfulness.
a phrase or expression where the meaning is not obvious from the individual words (e.g., 'kick the bucket' means to die).

explain_again: This page is like a word treasure hunt! We find lots of different words to say if someone is happy or grumpy. We also remember our poetry tricks like words that start with the same sound (alliteration) or words that rhyme. And we learn about 'idioms,' which are secret code phrases like 'it's raining cats and dogs' – it doesn't mean animals are falling, just that it's raining a lot!
fun_analogy: Using different adjectives for moods is like having a whole box of crayons instead of just two. You can colour in 'happy' with bright yellow, 'exuberant' with sparkling glitter, and 'grouchy' with a dark, scribbly grey. Idioms are like secret handshakes – you have to know the special meaning, you can't just guess!
